title: $\tau$-Voice: Benchmarking Full-Duplex Voice Agents on Real-World Domains
abstract: Full-duplex voice agents—systems that listen and speak simultaneously—are rapidly moving from research to production. However, existing evaluations address conversational dynamics and task completion in isolation. We introduce τ-Voice, a benchmark for evaluating voice agents on grounded tasks with real-world complexity: agents must navigate complex multi-turn conversations, adhere to domain policies, and interact with the environment. The framework extends τ²-bench into a novel voice agent benchmark combining verifiable completion of complex grounded tasks, full-duplex interaction, and realistic audio—enabling direct comparison between voice and text performance. A controllable and realistic voice user simulator provides diverse accents, realistic audio environments, and rich turn-taking dynamics; by decoupling simulation from wall-clock time, the user simulator can use the most capable LLM without real-time constraints. We evaluate task completion (pass@1) and voice interaction quality across 278 tasks: while GPT-5 (reasoning) achieves 85%, voice agents reach only 31–51% under clean conditions and 26–38% under realistic conditions with noise and diverse accents—retaining only 30–45% of text capability; qualitative analysis confirms 79–90% of failures stem from agent behavior, suggesting that observed failures primarily reflect agent behavior under our evaluation setup. τ-Voice provides a reproducible testbed for measuring progress toward voice agents that are natural, conversational, and reliable.
decision_comment:
There was some disagreement from reviewers, with one reviewer calling for reject and one calling for accept, two calling for weak accept.
I found the reviewer calling for reject didn't provide solid enough reasoning for the score, where the positive reviewers were more convincing, despite all reviewers finding some methodological weaknesses in the approach.

Accordingly, weak accept seems the right score.

reviews.summary: This paper introduces $\tau$-Voice, a benchmark for full-duplex voice agents on grounded customer-service tasks. It extends τ²-Bench with a full-duplex orchestrator, a controllable voice user simulator with accents/noise/turn-taking behaviors, and evaluation of both task completion and voice interaction quality over 278 tasks in retail, airline, and telecom. The main empirical claim is a large gap between text and voice performance, with voice systems reaching only 29–42% pass@1 in clean settings and 19–30% in realistic settings, versus 80% for the GPT-5 text baseline.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths
1. Benchmark intersection is genuinely novel.
2. The benchmark has 278 inherited grounded task, and the central contribution lies in the simulator, full-duplex orchestration, controllable realism, and dual evaluation axes.
3. The simulator design exposes accents, environmental noise, packet loss, muffling, backchannels, interruptions, and non-agent-directed speech in a controlled way, and the evaluation goes beyond pass@1 to include responsiveness, latency, interruption rate, and selectivity.

Weaknesses
1. The benchmark evaluates English only, uses TTS rather than recorded human speech, feeds the agent transcript directly to the user simulator instead of making the simulator perceive the agent only through speech, and uses a simulator that the paper itself acknowledges is more patient than real users.
2. It covers only three customer-service domains, inherits its task structure from $\tau^{2}$-Bench, and evaluates only three proprietary audio-native providers. There are no open-source or cascaded ASR→LLM→TTS baselines in the main experiments, even though the paper itself notes this as future work. That makes it harder to tell whether the measured gap is really about “voice agents” broadly, or mostly about a very specific set of frontier end-to-end APIs under one simulation regime.
3. The paper says task completion is “fully verifiable,” but for spoken interactions it uses LLM evaluation to verify some agent communications instead of exact matching, which introduces judge dependence.
4. Statistical reliability is also somewhat limited: repeated runs and confidence intervals are reported explicitly only for Retail, not for the full benchmark.
5. Some numbers computed in the Table are wrong and a Figure is placed without being referred.
reviews.key_questions_for_authors:
1. Figure 2 is not referred in the paper, and numbers in Table 7 seem to be wrongly computed, please check all Tables again and make the right claim for the manuscript.
2. Why are cascaded baselines such as ASR→LLM→TTS absent from the main comparison, given that the paper explicitly argues future work should include them? Without such baselines, it is difficult to know whether the reported gap is due to the voice modality itself or current end-to-end API design choices. It would be great if authors can provide a simple investigation in the rebuttal section.
3. The simulator receives the agent transcript directly rather than perceiving only the generated speech. Can the authors better quantify how much this simplification changes outcomes?
4. The paper claims novelty from combining three axes. Could the authors explicitly compare $\tau$-Voice against VoiceAgentBench, and articulate what benchmark capability τ-Voice adds that those frameworks do not already provide?
5. Why are repeated runs and confidence intervals emphasized only for Retail?
reviews.limitations: Its realism is partial rather than end-to-end: it is English-only, TTS-based, uses direct transcript injection on the agent-to-user side, and relies on a simulator that is more patient and more capable than real callers, which likely makes the setting easier and cleaner than real deployment. The benchmark is also narrower than the title may suggest, since it remains confined to three inherited customer-service domains and three proprietary audio-native APIs, with no main-result comparison to cascaded or open systems. Some core evaluation choices. especially LLM-based communication verification, limited repeated-run statistics outside Retail, and small-sample qualitative analysis. reduce methodological confidence.

reviews.summary: The paper introduces τ-voice, a benchmark for evaluating full-duplex voice agents (systems that listen and speak simultaneously) on complex, grounded real-world tasks. The benchmark extends τ²-bench to include multi-turn conversations, domain policy constraints, environmental interactions, and realistic audio conditions such as noise and diverse accents. Using a controllable voice user simulator, the authors evaluate 278 tasks and find that while text-based GPT-5 achieves about 80% task success, current voice agents reach only 29–42% in clean conditions and 19–30% in realistic settings, revealing a 50–61 percentage point performance gap. The results suggest most failures stem from agent behavior rather than evaluation artifacts, highlighting the significant challenges remaining for reliable voice agents.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Weaknesses:

(1) The paper appears incomplete in its current form. The related work section is limited and does not cover several recently released full-duplex voice models, which are highly relevant to the scope of this benchmark.

(2) Although the title suggests that the benchmark is specifically designed for full-duplex voice agents, the paper does not clearly explain what aspects of the evaluation are uniquely tailored to full-duplex interaction. From the current description, it is unclear how the proposed evaluation differs from benchmarks for general voice agents, or what specific components explicitly assess full-duplex capabilities.

(3) The paper proposes integrating evaluations across multiple stages as a new evaluation protocol. However, the motivation and novelty of this design remain unclear. It would be helpful for the authors to clarify why this integration is necessary and what new insights it provides beyond existing evaluation frameworks.

(4) The experimental evaluation includes only a small number of baselines. For a benchmark paper, it would be important to include a broader set of representative and competitive models, especially recent voice and multimodal agents, in order to provide a more comprehensive comparison.
reviews.key_questions_for_authors:
(1) The paper claims that τ-voice is specifically designed for full-duplex voice agents. Could the authors clarify which components of the benchmark explicitly evaluate full-duplex capabilities (e.g., simultaneous speaking/listening, interruption handling, overlap management), and how these differ from evaluations designed for general voice agents?

(2) How were the 278 tasks constructed? Are they derived from real-world applications or manually designed scenarios? It would be helpful to understand how representative they are of practical voice assistant usage.

(3) The benchmark relies heavily on a voice user simulator. How realistic is the simulator compared to real human conversations, particularly in terms of turn-taking behavior, interruptions, and conversational disfluencies?

(4) Since the simulator uses a strong LLM without real-time constraints, could this result in unrealistically capable users, potentially making the tasks more difficult than typical real-world interactions?

reviews.summary: This study proposes a novel benchmark called $\tau$-Voice, which, unlike existing approaches that separately evaluate content or task performance and behavioral dynamics in full-duplex spoken dialogue models, assesses both aspects in a unified manner. By segmenting continuous audio streams into 200ms units called Ticks and introducing a Full-Duplex Orchestrator that decouples real-world wall-clock time from simulated audio timestamps, the framework disregards the latency inherently arising from the LLM and TTS pipeline when incorporating actual speech into the evaluation, and instead adjusts simulated timestamps to conduct evaluations that faithfully reflect real-world conditions. Under this framework, the authors implement and compare three closed-source full-duplex voice interaction models, demonstrating that significant limitations still remain in full-duplex interaction.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
## Strengths
1. The approach of feeding the evaluated model's intermediate text to an LLM to generate inputs, thereby enabling the simulator to substitute for a human participant in the conversation, is well conceived.

2. Additionally, the decision to nullify the latency arising during the input speech generation process, in accordance with the fact that the actual models operate based on audio timestamps, appears to be effective.

3. Unlike prior work that evaluates each aspect separately across multiple benchmarks, this study enables the assessment of multiple dimensions simultaneously within a single unified benchmark.


## Weaknesses
1. Conversely, for the above approach to hold, the models being evaluated must necessarily output intermediate text responses and support operation based on audio timestamps rather than real-world physical time. In other words, the generalizability of this benchmark and evaluation pipeline may vary depending on the form in which future models are released, which appears to be a limitation.

2. Furthermore, the scope of the evaluation leans toward practical, task-specific aspects. Even considering just the performance results reported by the authors, there clearly remains a long way to go. It seems inevitable that as models move toward full-duplex, end-to-end architectures, they lose a substantial degree of the general backbone LLM's capabilities, let alone task-specific proficiency. Therefore, it would have been preferable if the evaluation axes included not only domain-specific tasks but also broader assessments of general commonsense conversational ability, especially given the current pace and state of research in this area.

3. Among representative open-source models, Moshi, PersonaPlex, and MiniCPM-o 4.5 appear to be notable examples. Considering the paper submission timeline and the release dates of the other models, it seems that, at the very least, an evaluation of whether this benchmark can be applied to Moshi, along with the corresponding results, would have enriched the paper further.

reviews.summary: This paper introduces τ-Voice, a novel benchmark designed to evaluate full-duplex voice agents—AI systems capable of listening and speaking simultaneously—within complex, real-world task environments. Unlike prior evaluation frameworks that separately measure conversational ability or task completion, τ-Voice integrates grounded multi-turn dialogue, domain-specific policy adherence, environmental interaction, and realistic audio input into a unified assessment framework. The benchmark extends τ²-bench by incorporating a controllable voice user simulator that generates diverse accents, background noise conditions, and natural turn-taking dynamics, enabling direct comparison between text-based and voice-based agent performance without real-time constraints. Experimental evaluation across 278 tasks reveals a substantial performance gap between state-of-the-art reasoning models and current voice agents, with task completion rates dropping significantly under realistic audio conditions. Qualitative analysis further shows that most observed failures arise from agent behavioral limitations rather than simulation artifacts, demonstrating the need for improved conversational robustness in deployed voice systems.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths

A key strength of this work lies in its holistic and reproducible evaluation framework that bridges the gap between conversational fluency and grounded task execution in voice-based AI systems. By combining verifiable task completion metrics with realistic full-duplex audio interaction, τ-Voice moves beyond traditional text-centric benchmarks and captures practical challenges faced in real-world deployments, such as noisy environments, accent variability, and dynamic turn-taking. The introduction of a decoupled, high-fidelity voice user simulator allows for scalable testing using advanced language models without latency constraints, enhancing both realism and experimental control. Additionally, the benchmark’s ability to directly compare voice and text agent performance provides valuable diagnostic insight into modality-specific weaknesses, offering a meaningful foundation for future research on improving reliability, naturalness, and behavioral robustness in next-generation voice agents.

Weaknesses

No major weaknesses were identified in this work. However, there are a few minor points that could be clarified or improved to further strengthen the paper. 

First, in the experimental results table, each evaluated model is labeled according to its provider (e.g., OpenAI, Google, xAI). While this categorization may offer high-level context, it would be more appropriate to compare performance primarily based on individual model names rather than their providers, as the benchmark aims to assess model capability rather than organizational origin. The rationale for presenting results in terms of providers was not clearly explained in the main text; therefore, it would be helpful if the authors could clarify whether there is a specific methodological reason for this design choice. 

Second, although relatively minor, the Limitations and Future Work sections are presented in a rather abstract and concise manner. Providing a more detailed discussion of the benchmark’s current constraints—such as simulation fidelity, domain coverage, or evaluation assumptions—as well as outlining more concrete future research directions would improve transparency and offer valuable guidance for subsequent work building upon this benchmark.
